does: This paper introduces Hallucination-targeted Direct Preference Optimization (HDPO), a method that applies preference learning to reduce hallucinations in multimodal large language models. Rather than treating hallucination as a monolithic problem, the authors develop three specialized types of preference pair datasets targeting distinct causes: insufficient visual understanding capabilities, challenges in long-context generation, and conflicts between visual and textual modalities. The method leverages direct preference optimization to train MLLMs to prefer accurate, grounded responses over hallucinated ones. Experiments across multiple hallucination benchmarks demonstrate that HDPO achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance, with ablation studies confirming that each category of preference pairs contributes meaningfully to reducing specific hallucination types.
connects: This work builds on the recent surge in applying preference optimization techniques to language models, particularly Direct Preference Optimization which has shown success in aligning LLMs with human preferences. The paper extends this line of work to the multimodal domain where hallucinations present unique challenges due to the interaction between vision and language components. It responds to earlier attempts to use DPO for MLLMs that showed inconsistent results, recognizing that generic preference learning may not address the heterogeneous nature of hallucination causes. The research also connects to prior work categorizing hallucination types in vision-language models and the broader literature on vision-language alignment, building on established understanding that hallucinations stem from visual grounding failures, sequence modeling issues, and modality conflicts.
enables: This work enables more targeted interventions for hallucination mitigation in multimodal systems by demonstrating that cause-specific preference learning is more effective than generic approaches. The framework suggests future research could decompose other model failure modes into constituent causes amenable to specialized preference optimization. The method potentially opens directions for developing diagnostic tools that identify which types of hallucinations a model exhibits, enabling selective application of remedies. It also points toward hybrid training strategies combining multiple preference signals and could inspire similar cause-targeted approaches in other multimodal tasks beyond hallucination reduction, such as improving coherence between modalities or enhancing factual grounding in vision-language applications.
